name: Mahayana Buddhism
shortDescription: Mahayana Buddhism, also known as the "Great Vehicle," is one of the two main branches of Buddhism. It emphasizes the path of the Bodhisattva, striving for enlightenment not only for oneself but for the benefit of all sentient beings.
description: Mahayana Buddhism, emerging around the 1st century CE, represents a significant evolution from earlier Buddhist schools. Its core tenet revolves around the Bodhisattva ideal – individuals who postpone their own final liberation (Nirvana) to help others achieve enlightenment. This contrasts with the Theravada tradition, which emphasizes individual liberation. Key concepts in Mahayana include emptiness (sunyata), the idea that all phenomena lack inherent existence; Buddha-nature (tathagatagarbha), the inherent potential for enlightenment within all beings; and the concept of multiple Buddhas and Bodhisattvas residing in various Buddha-fields. Mahayana scriptures are vast and diverse, including the Lotus Sutra, the Heart Sutra, and the Diamond Sutra. These texts often employ skillful means (upaya) to convey profound teachings in accessible ways. The doctrine of the Trikaya, the three bodies of the Buddha (Dharmakaya, Sambhogakaya, and Nirmanakaya), is also central to Mahayana cosmology, explaining the different ways the Buddha manifests. Mahayana Buddhism spread widely throughout Asia, particularly in China, Korea, Japan, Vietnam, and Tibet, developing into various schools and traditions, each with its own unique interpretations and practices. Zen, Pure Land, and Tibetan Buddhism are all examples of Mahayana traditions.
